source: McNeese & Hoag, Engineering and Technical Handbook, Table 11
description: Volume, surface area, centroid, and moments of inertia for standard 3D solids
notation: M = total mass (uniformly distributed), all moments of inertia about centroidal axes unless noted, k = radius of gyration = sqrt(I/M)

bodies.name: Rectangular parallelepiped (box)
bodies.shape_description: Solid rectangular box with edge lengths a, b, c aligned along x, y, z axes respectively

centroid.reference: corner of the box at origin

bodies.parameters: a = length along x, b = length along y, c = length along z
bodies.nasa_application: Satellite bus structures, electronic equipment boxes, solar panel substrates, payload fairings (rectangular cross-section), cargo modules
bodies.notes: For a cube (a = b = c), all moments equal M·a²/6. The parallel axis theorem shifts I to any parallel axis: I' = I + Md².

bodies.name: Right circular cylinder
bodies.shape_description: Solid cylinder of radius r and height h, with the z-axis along the cylinder axis

centroid.reference: center of base circle at origin, z-axis along cylinder axis

bodies.parameters: r = radius of circular cross-section, h = height (axial length)
bodies.nasa_application: Rocket body stages, solid rocket boosters, cylindrical fuel tanks, centrifuge modules, habitat pressure vessels (ISS modules)
bodies.notes: The dominant shape in rocketry. A thin-walled cylinder (shell) has Iz = Mr² and Ix = M(6r² + h²)/12. For h >> r, Ix approaches Mh²/12 (a slender rod).

bodies.name: Hollow cylinder (pipe)
bodies.shape_description: Thick-walled cylindrical tube with outer radius R, inner radius r, and height h

centroid.reference: center of base annulus at origin, z-axis along cylinder axis

bodies.parameters: R = outer radius, r = inner radius, h = height (axial length)
bodies.nasa_application: Rocket body tubes, pressure vessel walls, turbopump casings, heat exchangers, structural tubes in trusses (ISS truss segments)
bodies.notes: As r approaches R (thin wall), Iz approaches MR² and this becomes a thin cylindrical shell. Stiffness-to-weight ratio is superior to solid cylinder for structural applications.

bodies.name: Right circular cone
bodies.shape_description: Solid cone with base radius r and height h, apex at top, base in xy-plane

centroid.reference: center of base circle at origin, apex at z = h

bodies.parameters: r = base radius, h = height from base to apex, slant height l = sqrt(r² + h²)
bodies.nasa_application: Nose cones and heat shields, reentry vehicle shapes, rocket nozzle exit cones, payload fairings (conical sections), Apollo command module shape
bodies.notes: Centroid is at h/4 from the base (3/4 of the way from apex). The Ix_apex formula gives the moment about the apex — useful for spin-stabilized reentry vehicles. For a half-angle cone: tan(alpha) = r/h.

bodies.name: Frustum of a cone
bodies.shape_description: Truncated cone with larger base radius R, smaller top radius r, and height h between parallel faces

centroid.reference: center of large base at origin, small base at z = h

moments_of_inertia.notes_on_Iz: About the axis of symmetry. For transverse axes, use integration or subtract cone moments.

bodies.parameters: R = large base radius, r = small base radius, h = perpendicular height between bases, slant height l = sqrt((R - r)² + h²)
bodies.nasa_application: Rocket interstage adapters, payload adapter rings, nozzle extensions, spacecraft bus transition sections, launch escape tower fairings
bodies.notes: When r = 0, this reduces to a complete cone. When r = R, it becomes a cylinder. The centroid formula reduces to h/4 from the base when r = 0 (cone) and h/2 when r = R (cylinder). The Iz formula uses the relation V_frustum = V_large_cone - V_small_cone.

bodies.name: Sphere
bodies.shape_description: Solid sphere of radius r centered at origin
bodies.nasa_application: Spherical fuel tanks (LOX/LH2), pressure vessels, reaction control propellant tanks, communications antenna radomes, planetary bodies (gravitational modeling)
bodies.notes: Moments of inertia are equal about all axes through the center — maximum symmetry. A solid sphere has I = 2Mr²/5. Compare with thin spherical shell: I = 2Mr²/3. The sphere encloses maximum volume for minimum surface area.

bodies.name: Hollow sphere (spherical shell)
bodies.shape_description: Thick-walled spherical shell with outer radius R and inner radius r
bodies.parameters: R = outer radius, r = inner radius
bodies.nasa_application: Spherical pressure vessels (propellant tanks), reaction control system tanks, planetary interior modeling (layered core/mantle), crew module pressure shells
bodies.notes: As r approaches 0, reduces to solid sphere (I = 2MR²/5). As r approaches R (thin shell), I approaches 2MR²/3. The thin shell limit is useful for quick estimates of tank rotational inertia.

bodies.name: Hemisphere
bodies.shape_description: Solid half-sphere of radius r, flat face in the xy-plane

centroid.reference: center of flat base at origin, curved surface extends in +z direction

bodies.parameters: r = radius of the sphere from which the hemisphere is cut
bodies.nasa_application: Dome ends of cylindrical pressure vessels, observatory domes, hemispherical antenna reflectors, heat shield shapes, tank bulkheads
bodies.notes: Centroid is at 3r/8 above the flat face. Iz (about the axis of symmetry) equals that of a full sphere of the same radius. Ix about the base = 2Mr²/5 by symmetry of the integration. The Ix about the centroid uses the parallel axis theorem: Ix_centroid = 2Mr²/5 - M(3r/8)² = M(83r²/320).

bodies.name: Ellipsoid
bodies.shape_description: Solid ellipsoid with semi-axes a, b, c along the x, y, z axes: x²/a² + y²/b² + z²/c² = 1
bodies.surface_area: No closed-form expression in elementary functions (approximations exist; Knud Thomsen: S ≈ 4π((a^p·b^p + a^p·c^p + b^p·c^p)/3)^(1/p) where p ≈ 1.6075)
bodies.parameters: a = semi-axis along x, b = semi-axis along y, c = semi-axis along z
bodies.nasa_application: Oblate planet modeling (Earth: a = b > c), prolate spacecraft shapes, ellipsoidal fuel tank heads, gravitational potential modeling of asteroids and moons
bodies.notes: When a = b = c = r, reduces to a sphere with I = 2Mr²/5. An oblate spheroid (a = b > c) models planetary flattening. Earth's oblateness (f ≈ 1/298.257) affects satellite orbit precession (J2 perturbation). The surface area has no elementary closed form — this is one of the classic unsolved problems in elementary geometry.

bodies.name: Paraboloid of revolution
bodies.shape_description: Solid formed by rotating y = sqrt(x) (or z² = (r²/h)·x) about its axis, from vertex to height h, with base radius r

centroid.reference: vertex of paraboloid at origin, axis along z, base at z = h

bodies.parameters: r = radius of base circle, h = height from vertex to base
bodies.nasa_application: Parabolic antenna reflectors (Deep Space Network), solar concentrators, rocket nozzle bell contour approximation, searchlight reflectors on spacecraft
bodies.notes: The centroid is at 2h/3 from the vertex (h/3 from the base). Volume is exactly half that of the circumscribing cylinder. Parabolic reflectors focus parallel rays to a single point — the principle behind DSN's 70m dishes. Iz about the axis = Mr²/3 (compare cylinder Mr²/2).

bodies.name: Torus
bodies.shape_description: Solid torus (doughnut) generated by rotating a circle of radius r about an axis at distance R from the circle center (R > r)

centroid.reference: center of the torus at origin, torus lies in the xy-plane

bodies.parameters: R = distance from center of torus to center of tube (major radius), r = radius of the tube cross-section (minor radius), R > r for a ring torus
bodies.nasa_application: O-ring seals (Challenger SRB joints), toroidal propellant tanks (proposed), space station habitat ring concepts, magnetic confinement geometries (fusion research)
bodies.notes: Pappus' theorem gives both volume and surface area directly: V = 2πR × πr² (centroid path × cross-section area), S = 2πR × 2πr (centroid path × circumference). Iz is about the axis of symmetry (z-axis perpendicular to the torus plane) — reduces to MR² for a thin ring (r → 0). Ix = Iy are about diameters in the torus plane — reduce to MR²/2 for a thin ring.

bodies.name: Right prism (general)
bodies.shape_description: Solid prism with arbitrary cross-section of area A and perimeter P, extruded to height h along the z-axis

centroid.reference: base cross-section in xy-plane at z = 0, z-axis along prism axis

moments_of_inertia.note: Ix_area and Iy_area are the second moments of area of the cross-section about its centroidal axes

bodies.parameters: A = cross-sectional area, P = perimeter of cross-section, h = height (length), Ix_area and Iy_area = second moments of area of the cross-section
bodies.nasa_application: Structural beams and longerons, extruded truss members, hexagonal satellite bus sections, triangular cross-section stiffeners
bodies.notes: This is the general form — substitute the specific cross-section properties from the plane areas table (engineering-tables.json). For example, a triangular prism with base b and height d has A = bd/2, Ix_area = bd³/36. The mass moments use the relation: I_mass = (M/A) × I_area for the transverse components plus the axial h²/12 term.

bodies.name: Pyramid (general)
bodies.shape_description: Solid pyramid with arbitrary polygonal base of area A and height h, tapering to an apex
bodies.surface_area: A + sum of lateral face areas (depends on base shape)

centroid.reference: centroid of base at (x_bar, y_bar, 0), apex at z = h

moments_of_inertia.note: For a square base (side a): Iz_centroid = Ma²/10, Ix_centroid = M(a²/20 + 3h²/80)

radius_of_gyration.note: Depends on base shape — see specific cases below

bodies.parameters: A = base area, h = perpendicular height from base to apex
bodies.nasa_application: Pyramidal truss nodes, tetrahedral spacecraft structures (TDRS antenna feeds), launch escape system aerodynamic surfaces
bodies.notes: Centroid is always at h/4 from the base, regardless of base shape — this is a general result. Volume is always Ah/3. For a right pyramid with square base (side a): V = a²h/3, centroid at h/4, Iz = Ma²/10 about the axis of symmetry.

bodies.name: Wedge
bodies.shape_description: Solid wedge formed by cutting a rectangular parallelepiped diagonally — rectangular base of dimensions a (length) and b (width), with height h tapering linearly to zero along one edge
bodies.surface_area: ab/2 + bh/2 + b·sqrt(a² + h²)/2 + ah (two triangular faces + sloped face + rectangular base... depends on specific wedge orientation)

centroid.reference: corner where height = h at origin, a along x (tapering to zero height), b along y, h along z

moments_of_inertia.note: These are approximate forms for a symmetric wedge. Exact values depend on the specific wedge geometry.

bodies.parameters: a = length of base along tapering direction, b = width of base (constant), h = maximum height
bodies.nasa_application: Aerodynamic control surfaces (fins, vanes), structural gussets, spacecraft docking adapter wedge rings, thermal protection tile shapes on orbiter leading edges
bodies.notes: A wedge is half of a rectangular parallelepiped cut along a diagonal. Volume = abh/6 (one-sixth of the circumscribing box). The centroid at x = a/4 means the mass center is shifted toward the thick end. For a symmetric wedge (isosceles triangular cross-section), use the triangular prism formulas.

bodies.name: Regular tetrahedron
bodies.shape_description: Solid regular tetrahedron with all four faces equilateral triangles of edge length a

centroid.reference: one vertex at origin — centroid is equidistant from all four vertices at distance a√6/4 from each vertex

bodies.nasa_application: Tetrahedral satellite configurations (cluster missions like MMS — Magnetospheric Multiscale), structural nodes in truss systems, tetrahedral mesh elements in FEA
bodies.notes: All moments of inertia about centroidal axes are equal (Ma²/20) due to the tetrahedral symmetry. Height of regular tetrahedron: h = a√(2/3). The centroid divides each median in ratio 3:1 from vertex. Volume can also be written as √2·a³/12 ≈ 0.1178·a³. The inscribed sphere has radius r = a/(2√6), the circumscribed sphere has radius R = a√6/4.
